nativeRange.habitat: The Indian Green Shrimp is found in slow-moving, vegetation-rich streams and rivers across central India and Malaysia, with some records suggesting the range extends westward as far as Iraq. It favors sheltered, planted waters where biofilm and leaf litter are abundant.

diet.notes: Indian Green Shrimp are excellent grazers of algae and biofilm, which forms the bulk of their diet in a well-established tank. They also accept blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, carrot, and spinach, as well as Indian almond and alder leaves and standard sinking shrimp pellets or wafers. A mature tank with a good biofilm layer meets most of their nutritional needs, particularly for newly hatched fry. Feed sparingly — uneaten food left in the tank can quickly foul the water.

goodWith: small peaceful nano fish (chili rasboras, ember tetras, pygmy corydoras)
other dwarf shrimp (Amano, Bamboo, Tangerine Tiger, Malawa)
avoidWith: shrimp predators (most cichlids, gouramis, larger barbs)
Neocaridina species in breeding setups (no hybridisation but visually confusing colonies)

summary: The Indian Green Shrimp is a small, color-changing Caridina native to streams in India and Malaysia, sold in green, blue, brown, and striped color forms including the 'Malaya' and 'Indian Zebra' morphs. It is generally regarded as more tolerant of a wider range of water parameters than Crystal Red or Crystal Black Shrimp, making it one of the more approachable Caridina species for hobbyists.
careNotes: The scientific name Caridina cf. babaulti refers to a taxonomic complex of closely related shrimp distributed across central India through Malaysia; the 'cf.' notation signals that formal classification of the group remains unresolved. In the aquarium trade, this complex is sold under several overlapping names — Green Shrimp, Malaya Shrimp, Blue Malaya, Stripe Malaya, and Indian Zebra Shrimp — that may all represent the same biological species expressing different color phases. One of the more striking traits of this shrimp is its ability to shift color rapidly in response to mood, substrate color, and lighting conditions, a behavior that sets it apart from most color morphs of the commonly kept Neocaridina davidi. The Indian Green Shrimp is commonly considered hardier and more tolerant of a wider range of water parameters than Crystal Red or Crystal Black Shrimp (also known as Bee Shrimp), which require very soft, acidic water to thrive. Even so, newly imported specimens are often reported as fragile, and the species tends to do best in mature, biologically stable tanks where ammonia and nitrite remain undetectable and nitrate is kept ideally under 10 ppm. Like all dwarf shrimp, this species is extremely sensitive to copper, which is present in many common fish medications, plant fertilisers, and snail treatments — any product containing copper should be kept out of a tank housing these shrimp. The Indian Green Shrimp will not hybridise with Neocaridina davidi color morphs, but different color forms within the babaulti complex can interbreed, so hobbyists wishing to maintain a consistent color line should keep only one color form per breeding tank.
breedingNotes: Breeding is generally considered easy to moderate in a mature, stable tank. Females carry roughly 30 to 60 eggs (some sources report up to 80) in clusters beneath the tail, and the eggs typically hatch after about 30 days. Unlike many aquatic invertebrates, the young emerge as fully formed miniature shrimp with no free-swimming larval stage. The newly hatched fry are smaller than those of the more commonly kept Neocaridina davidi and depend heavily on biofilm and fine algae in the first weeks of life, making a well-established tank with mature substrate particularly important. Females are noticeably larger and rounder than males and often display a visible 'saddle' — an egg mass visible through the body — before spawning. Sponge filters are recommended in breeding tanks, as standard power filter intakes can draw in and injure fry. Juveniles are reported to reach sexual maturity at around 75 days, at a size of roughly 2 to 2.5 cm. This species does not crossbreed with Neocaridina davidi or its color morphs.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Primary reference for general husbandry and parameter targets (pH 6.8-7.8, gH 4-14, temp 75-82 °F). Direct fetch returned 403 so content was reached via search-engine summaries and corroborating retailer pages.

additional.name: Flip Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-check for water parameters (pH 7.0-7.6, gH 7-15, kH 2-8, TDS 150-350), tank size (4 gal minimum, 20 gal recommended), and Malaya morph identification. Notes Caridina babaulti is 'much hardier than a typical Caridina'. Temperature stated as 68-72 °F is unusually low vs other sources; averaged with consensus 75-82 °F to 22-28 °C in this entry.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-check for taxonomy (family Atyidae), native range (central India to Malaya, west to Iraq), size (2.5-4 cm), lifespan (1-2 years), color variants (Green / Stripes / Malaya / Brown / Red / Yellow / Blue), and breeding (30-day incubation, ~75-day maturity). Confirms color-change behavior and lack of Neocaridina hybridisation.

additional.name: Garnelio (EN)
additional.notes: Cross-check for temperature (24-28 °C), pH (7-7.5), size (~2.5 cm females), lifespan (18-24 months), and minimum tank 10-20 L. Confirms Malaysia as a native range and the white-striped Malaya color pattern.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Cross-check for copper sensitivity warning, breeding (30-50 eggs, ~30-day hatch), and Green color phase. Confirms maximum size of about 1.4 in (~3.5 cm).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts periodically as the shrimp grows; discarded exuviae are typically left in the tank as they re-mineralize the water and are sometimes eaten. Females usually molt just before mating, when they release pheromones that trigger a brief 'shrimp run' by males. Stable parameters (especially gH and kH) are important for clean molts; sudden swings can cause stuck molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Breeds readily in mature, stable freshwater tanks with abundant biofilm. Eggs incubate for approximately 30 days and hatch directly into miniature adults, with no planktonic larval stage. To maintain a consistent color morph, keep only one color form per breeding tank, as different color phases can interbreed.
